transcript: keeva okafor room 7 right so this is keeva okafor in room seven morning shift handover assessment starting at approximately seven fifteen on thursday. keeva had an extremely disturbed night again again she was up from the bed at least four separate times according to the night documentation written by assumpta. on each occasion she was found attempting to walk to the toilet unassisted despite her falls prevention care plan which clearly states she must use the call bell and wait for assistance before standing. the sensor mat alarm activated on three of the four occasions but on the second occasion at approximately one forty five in the morning assumpta noted in the clinical notes that the sensor mat was found folded at the foot of the bed suggesting keeva had deliberately repositioned it. this is the same behaviour she demonstrated last monday and last wednesday and it constitutes a recurring pattern of deliberate circumvention of her safety equipment. i have documented this as a recurring safety incident and i will flag it with the unit manager and with the physiotherapist for urgent review of the falls prevention care plan. keeva was found standing in the centre of her room at approximately three twenty in the morning without her zimmer frame and without her non slip footwear. assumpta guided her safely back to bed and completed a post standing assessment finding no injury but keeva was very agitated and was saying she needed to find her sister nora. nora passed away in two thousand and nine and keeva frequently calls for her during periods of nocturnal confusion which is well documented in her dementia care plan. her sundowning behaviour has been escalating over the past two weeks and i have noted this pattern in the clinical notes and escalated to the consultant geriatrician dr flanagan who reviewed her on tuesday. dr flanagan at that review documented that keeva has moderate to severe alzheimers disease and that her nocturnal behavioural disturbances are consistent with disease progression. she did not prescribe any new medication at that point but said she would review again in two weeks. keeva is on warfrin three milligrams daily for her atrial fibrillation. her inr was checked last monday and returned at two point eight which is within her therapeutic target range of two to three. dr kelly reviewed this result and was happy with the control. however keeva refused refused refused her warfrin tablet again this morning along with her bisoprolol two point five milligrams and her lanzoprazole thirty milligrams. she pushed the medication cup away firmly and turned her face to the wall and said she was not sick and did not need tablets. this is now the fourth consecutive morning that she has refused these specific medications and i am deeply concerned about the implications of missed warfrin doses for her stroke risk given her atrial fibrillation. i attempted to offer the tablets again at seven forty five hidden in a small teaspoon of strawberry jam which is a strategy that has worked previously but this morning she spat out the jam and tablets together. i have documented this second refusal attempt and i have bleeped dr kelly to discuss the options which may include a liquid formulation of warfrin or a review of the appropriateness of anticoagulation given her refusal pattern and her increased falls risk. i have also completed a capacity assessment form in the notes as keevas repeated and consistent refusals may represent an expression of her current wishes rather than a capacity impaired decision and this distinction is clinically and ethically important. her blood pressure this morning at seven am was one hundred and ninety over ninety seven. i repeated it on her left arm at seven fifteen and got one hundred and eighty six over ninety three. both readings are significantly above her usual baseline of approximately one hundred and forty five over eighty five. i believe this hypertensive reading is directly related to her not having taken her bisoprolol last night at six pm or this morning given that the night nurse documents she also refused the evening medications yesterday. i have placed a call to dr kelly and have documented the blood pressure readings as requiring urgent review today. her respiratory rate this morning is seventeen breaths per minute and her oxygen saturations on room air are ninety six percent both of which are within normal limits for her. her pulse is eighty eight beats per minute and irregular which is her baseline atrial fibrillation. her temperature is thirty six point six celsius. her blood glucose is not routinely monitored as she is not diabetic. moving on to skin integrity i completed a full body skin assessment this morning during personal care. there is a new area of non-blanchable erythema measuring approximately two point five centimetres by two centimetres on her left heel which was not documented in yesterday's skin assessment. this constitutes a grade one pressure injury and i have applied a five layer foam heel protector dressing and documented this in the wound care chart. i have also elevated the left heel on a foam wedge pillow and i have updated the pressure relief care plan to include specific heel offloading at every repositioning episode. the duoderm sacral dressing which was applied on tuesday was found partially peeled at the edges this morning and i have replaced it with a fresh sacral foam dressing following a clean of the area. the skin underneath showed grade one redness which has not progressed to grade two so the dressing has been effective in preventing further deterioration. keeva took her breakfast this morning reasonably well considering her disturbed night. she ate approximately three quarters of her porridge with honey and took two full cups of tea and one hundred and fifty millilitres of orange juice. she is a red tray resident requiring full nursing assistance at mealtimes secondary to her parkinson's disease affecting her fine motor control. her swallow was last assessed by the speech and language therapy team in february and she is safe for normal textures with stage two thickened fluids. this morning i noticed she coughed on three consecutive sips of her thickened tea and once on a spoonful of porridge. this is a change from her recent pattern and i have documented a suspected swallowing deterioration and have put in an urgent salt referral requesting review within forty eight hours. her weight this morning is fifty one point two kilograms compared to fifty two point one kilograms on monday which is a loss of nine hundred grams in three days. i have referred this to the dietitian as unintentional weight loss exceeding five percent in one month and have documented it as a nutritional concern. her urine output was charted by the night nurse as approximately five hundred and forty millilitres across the eight hour night shift which is low and her urine this morning is concentrated dark amber colour suggesting dehydration. i have increased her oral fluid target to one thousand and five hundred millilitres for the day shift and i am tracking this on the fluid balance chart. i am also querying whether keeva has a urinary tract infection as her urine has a distinctly offensive odour this morning and she was more acutely confused than her usual baseline throughout the night and into this morning. i have sent a midstream urine sample for culture and sensitivity this morning. there is no temperature spike at present but temperature can be blunted in elderly people with dementia and a normal temperature does not rule out infection.

aishlin varghese room 12 okay so this is aishlin varghese in room twelve this is the late morning assessment being completed at approximately eleven fifty on tuesday. aishlins morning has been highly eventful and i need to document everything carefully for medical and safety reasons. at nine ten this morning my colleague brigid alerted me that the healthcare assistant deirdre had found aishlin actively pulling at her peg tube with her right hand and that the tube appeared to be displaced. i attended immediately and confirmed that the external bumper of the peg tube had moved approximately two point five centimetres outward from its usual position at skin level. there was a small amount of clear gastric fluid leaking around the stoma site. i stabilised the tube manually while brigid called the on call physician dr murphy who attended at nine twenty five. dr murphy confirmed the tube was partially dislodged and that the balloon or bumper was at risk of passing through the stoma if further tension was applied. he has documented that the tube is stable for now and does not require immediate surgical intervention but that interventional radiology review is recommended within twenty four hours to formally assess tube position and stoma integrity. a padded safety mitten has been applied to aishlins dominant right hand following a formal restraint assessment and with the agreement of her daughter maura who was contacted by phone at nine forty and gave verbal consent. a written consent form will be completed with maura when she visits this afternoon. this is documented as a restrictive practice intervention in accordance with the unit policy and it will be reviewed at the next multidisciplinary team meeting scheduled for friday morning. this is the fifth documented episode of peg tube interference by aishlin in the past fourteen days. the incidents are escalating in frequency and severity and the clinical team has been notified at each occurrence. the palliative care clinical nurse specialist catherine reviewed aishlin on friday last week in the context of these incidents and raised the question of whether continuing enteral feeding via peg tube remains in aishlin's best interest given her increasing resistance and distress around the tube. this conversation has been had with maura who is aishlin's next of kin and healthcare proxy. maura has asked for more time to consider the options and a formal best interest meeting has been scheduled for next monday at two pm with dr murphy, the palliative care consultant, the speech and language therapist, and the dietitian all attending. i have confirmed all attendees are available and the meeting is in the diary. aishlin had her peg feed running at seventy five millilitres per hour of fresubin original since midnight last night. following this morning's displacement event dr murphy has directed that the feed be held until the interventional radiology review is completed. she is receiving thirty millilitre water flushes every six hours through the tube to keep it patent but no further feed is running. her last blood glucose check at eleven thirty was nine point two millimoles per litre. she is on novorapid six units three times daily with feeds and lantus twelve units nightly. given that her feeds are held i am not administering the mealtime novorapid doses today as there is no associated carbohydrate load and i have specifically spoken with dr murphy about this. he has verbally authorised holding the novorapid for today with hourly blood glucose monitoring to detect hypoglycaemia. her eleven fifty glucose is eight point eight which is acceptable. at ten am her glucose was fourteen point two which was elevated and i queried stress hyperglycaemia given the distressing incident of the morning. the pattern of elevated morning glucose over the past week is documented and dr murphy has requested a diabetes specialist nurse review this week. aishlins bowels have not opened in five days today. her usual pattern is every two to three days. she is on lactulose fifteen millilitres twice daily and movicol one sachet daily as a maintenance laxative regime but this is clearly insufficient at present. i administered two glycerine suppositories at ten thirty this morning with aishlins consent and she tolerated this intervention without distress. at eleven forty five she passed a small amount of hard stool which provided some relief but her abdomen remains mildly distended and tympanic on percussion. i have documented this and asked the dietitian to review her fibre and fluid management. i have also increased her water flushes to eighty millilitres four times daily and i will recheck for bowel response this afternoon. aishlins communication remains significantly affected by her cerebrovascular accident seventeen months ago. she has predominantly expressive dysphasia with relatively preserved comprehension. this morning she became very distressed during the peg tube incident and was attempting to communicate something repeatedly that sounded like she was saying maura or door or more but i could not determine the exact meaning despite multiple attempts to clarify using her communication board. she eventually became too agitated to use the board effectively and began vocalising loudly and banging the cot side with her left hand. i sat with her for approximately thirty minutes and used a calm reassuring tone, physical proximity, and played her preferred irish traditional music from the radio in her room. her agitation gradually subsided to a manageable level over this period. the music intervention has been the most reliably effective non-pharmacological approach for her according to all nursing documentation over the past several months and it is documented as a priority in her dementia behaviour care plan. her blood pressure at midday was one hundred and fourteen over seventy two which is slightly below her usual baseline of one thirty over seventy five. i have checked lying and standing blood pressure and got one hundred and eight over sixty eight sitting up from lying suggesting mild orthostatic hypotension. i have documented this and all care staff have been verbally briefed that aishlin must not be transferred to her wheelchair without a slow graduated position change supervised by a registered nurse. the tissue viability nurse patricia reviewed the peg stoma site on thursday and recommended silver nitrate application for the perilesional granulation tissue. this was applied on thursday and monday. the granulation has reduced slightly but persists. patricia is aware of todays displacement event and has been contacted by phone. she will review the stoma in person tomorrow morning and will advise on further wound management. the wound assessment chart and photographs have been updated today.

shivawn pillai room 3A okay shivawn pillai room three a this is the afternoon assessment it is two fifteen on wednesday and i have quite a lot to cover today as shivawn has had a significant morning. at ten forty two this morning shivawn was found by the healthcare assistant patricia on the bathroom floor of her ensuite. she had apparently risen from the toilet unassisted and fallen backward striking her lower back against the edge of the bath before landing on the tiled floor. this is particularly concerning because shivawn is on a two person assist protocol for all transfers including toilet transfers and she was placed on this protocol following her last fall six weeks ago. the two person assist protocol requires that a nurse or healthcare assistant must be present and assisting at all times during transfers and the bed sensor alarm must be activated when she is in bed. the bed sensor alarm was not triggered this morning prior to this fall which suggests it may not have been correctly reactivated after her eight am personal care session. i am investigating this and have spoken with the morning shift nurse sinead and the healthcare assistant brigid who performed the morning care. both recall completing the morning routine but neither can confirm with certainty that the sensor was reactivated. i have escalated this to the unit manager and a full incident review is underway. the immediate clinical assessment of shivawn following the fall was completed at ten fifty by me in conjunction with the attending physician dr farrell who was present on the ward. there is no apparent fracture of the hip knees wrists or spine. shivawn is weight bearing on both legs with her usual zimmer frame though she is more guarded and moves more slowly than her normal baseline. there is a small contusion approximately four centimetres by three centimetres over the left posterior iliac crest which is tender to palpation but not associated with any deformity or crepitus. i have applied an ice pack for twenty minutes and will reassess the bruise at four pm. there is a superficial abrasion over the right knee approximately one point five centimetres in diameter which i cleaned with normal saline and dressed with a non-adherent dressing and a light crepe bandage to prevent further trauma. i am running neurological observations every hour for the first four hours post fall then every two hours for the next eight hours as per the post fall neurological observation protocol. her glasgow coma scale at two fifteen is fifteen of fifteen. her pupils are equal at four millimetres bilaterally and reactive to light both direct and consensual. she denies head pain, nausea, or visual disturbance. however as i have already noted shivawn has moderate vascular dementia and is a poor historian and i am not able to rely entirely on her self report. i have documented this clinical caveat in the post fall assessment form. the family notification was completed at eleven fifteen when i telephoned her son eoin who was deeply concerned and is visiting tonight. eoin asked specifically whether an x-ray would be required and i explained that this is a clinical decision for dr farrell who has indicated he will make a formal recommendation at the ward round tomorrow morning following clinical review. dr farrell has documented in the interim notes that he does not feel an urgent x-ray is indicated at this stage given the clinical findings but that the situation will be reviewed. shivawn is on rivaroxaban twenty milligrams daily for her non-valvular atrial fibrillation and this creates a significantly elevated bleeding risk in the context of any fall with potential head or abdominal trauma. i have flagged this explicitly in the incident documentation and in the medical handover notes. dr farrell is aware. shivawn also has type two diabetes managed with metfomin five hundred milligrams twice daily and gliclazide eighty milligrams once daily in the morning. her pre-lunch blood glucose at twelve thirty today was seventeen point four millimoles per litre. i asked her whether she ate breakfast and she said she could not remember which is typical of her memory pattern. the kitchen staff confirmed she received her full breakfast but it was returned largely uneaten. the elevated blood glucose in the context of reduced intake is slightly paradoxical and i have attributed this at least in part to the physiological stress hyperglycaemia response triggered by the fall and the associated pain. i have documented this and will recheck at four pm. the pre-dinner glucose will guide whether any dose adjustment is needed. shivawn has been very emotionally distressed since the fall. she has cried intermittently throughout the afternoon and has repeatedly asked for her husband seamus who died two years ago. seamus's death is documented as a sensitive topic in her dementia care plan with guidance that she should not be reminded of his death during acute periods of distress as this causes severe secondary psychological distress. instead i have been using a gentle validation approach acknowledging that seamus loves her and that he would want her to rest comfortably today. this approach has been moderately effective. i have also contacted the pastoral care coordinator to ask whether a visit from the chaplain today might be helpful for shivawn given her level of emotional distress. her skin on the coccyx at two pm today shows a pink non-blanchable area approximately three centimetres in diameter that was not present at yesterday's skin check. this is a grade one pressure injury likely related to her being more sedentary and spending longer in her chair due to the fall precautions. i have applied a thin bordered hydrofibre dressing to the coccyx as prevention and updated the repositioning chart. she is to be repositioned every two hours this afternoon and overnight. her urinary catheter which was inserted two weeks ago for retention secondary to her neurogenic bladder is draining dark amber concentrated urine this afternoon. the volume since six am this morning is only two hundred and eighty millilitres which is very low. i have encouraged oral fluid intake and shivawn has taken approximately six hundred millilitres of fluids so far today. i will track fluid balance carefully through the afternoon and evening and will contact dr farrell if output does not improve.

feekra krishnan room 9 this is feekra krishnan room nine it is eight am thursday early shift handover assessment. feekra had an extremely serious night and i need to document this comprehensively as it has clinical legal and care planning implications. feekra experienced an acute episode of respiratory decompensation at approximately three fifteen in the morning according to the night shift documentation by niamh. niamh found feekra sitting bolt upright in bed in obvious respiratory distress she described feekra as pale and sweating and visibly using her neck and intercostal muscles to breathe. feekra was unable to speak in full sentences and kept gripping the cot side. niamh immediately commenced four litres per minute of oxygen via nasal cannula and activated the emergency call system while simultaneously contacting the on call physician dr oconnor by phone. dr oconnor attended within approximately twelve minutes at around three twenty seven. on his arrival he found feekras oxygen saturations at eighty nine percent on the four litre nasal cannula which he considered inadequate and consistent with a significant acute decompensation. he increased the oxygen to eight litres per minute via a thirty five percent venturi mask and reassessed after fifteen minutes obtaining saturations of ninety four percent which he judged to be a meaningful improvement but still below her usual resting baseline of ninety five to ninety seven percent on room air. at this point dr oconnor ordered a portable chest x-ray which was completed in her room at approximately four am using the mobile radiology unit. the radiograph has now been reported by the consultant radiologist this morning as showing bilateral basal airspace opacification worse on the right than the left, small bilateral pleural effusions, and increased vascular markings consistent with cardiogenic pulmonary oedema in the setting of her known ischaemic cardiomyopathy and congestive cardiac failure. dr oconnor administered furesemide forty milligrams intravenously via feekras peripherally inserted central catheter at four fifteen in the morning. he also took blood for urgent biochemistry including urea and electrolytes, full blood count, troponin, brain natriuretic peptide, and blood gases. the blood gas results from four thirty am showed a ph of seven point thirty three a pco two of fifty one and a po two of eighty one on eight litres via venturi which dr oconnor described as a mild type two respiratory failure with compensated respiratory acidosis. by five thirty niamh documented that feekras breathing had eased noticeably. she was less distressed and was able to lie back slightly although she remained at about forty five degrees head elevation. her saturations at five thirty were ninety six percent on six litres via the venturi mask and dr oconnor was satisfied with this response to the furesemide. at my eight am assessment feekras condition is as follows. saturations are ninety five percent on six litres via the venturi mask. respiratory rate is twenty four breaths per minute which remains elevated above her usual fourteen to sixteen. she is still slightly breathless when talking but is able to complete about two thirds of a sentence before needing to pause to breathe which is an improvement on the overnight picture. her pulse is one hundred and four beats per minute and irregularly irregular which is her chronic atrial fibrillation but the rate is faster than her usual controlled rate of around seventy to eighty. her blood pressure is one hundred and sixty two over ninety eight which is elevated above her usual managed baseline of one thirty over eighty. her temperature is thirty seven point one celsius. heart sounds on auscultation include an irregular rhythm with a third heart sound gallop audible at the apex which is a classical sign of cardiac decompensation and was not present on the last formal cardiac assessment. i have documented this finding and flagged it to dr oconnor who says the consultant cardiologist dr walsh has been asked to review feekra today as an urgent inpatient consultation. feekras peripherally inserted central catheter is in her right antecubital fossa and was inserted one week ago for her intravenous furesemide management programme. i have checked the picc line site this morning. the exit site is clean and non-inflamed. there is no redness tracking along the arm and no tenderness on palpation above the insertion site. the dressing is intact and dry and was last changed on tuesday. the line is patent with a good blood return confirmed by aspirating two millilitres of dark venous blood before administering the morning dose of furesemide. a second dose of furesemide forty milligrams was administered via the picc at six am as per the overnight escalation prescription written by dr oconnor and a further forty milligrams is prescribed for twelve noon today. feekras urine output from her urethral catheter overnight from midnight to eight am was one thousand one hundred and twenty millilitres which represents an excellent diuretic response and confirms the furesemide is working. i changed the catheter bag at eight am and the current urine is clear and pale yellow suggesting improving hydration status. the fluid balance for the past twenty four hours shows she is now approximately negative four hundred millilitres which is appropriate given the goal of diuresis for pulmonary congestion. i need to monitor closely that she does not become excessively fluid depleted and i will check her lying blood pressure and assess for signs of hypovolaemia at midday. feekras routine morning medications were reviewed carefully this morning. bisoprolol two point five milligrams has been withheld this morning pending dr oconnors review given the elevated heart rate and the concern that the beta blocker may be contributing to the cardiac decompensation in some patients. this is documented as a withheld medication with clinical reason. spironalactone twenty five milligrams was administered as prescribed. ramipril two point five milligrams was administered. her anticoagulant apixaban two point five milligrams twice daily was administered as prescribed as the risks of stroke from atrial fibrillation outweigh the bleeding risk in her current clinical context. feekra is a type one diabetic on a basal bolus regime. her fasting glucose this morning was four point eight millimoles per litre. she received novorapid four units with breakfast but her oral intake was very poor consisting only of half a glass of fortisip and a few spoonfuls of scrambled egg. i am monitoring closely for hypoglycaemia given the reduced carbohydrate intake relative to the insulin dose. her ten am blood glucose was six point two which is acceptable. feekra and her family were very distressed overnight. her daughter anitha was telephoned at approximately seven fifteen this morning by me. anitha was very upset and is coming in with her brother rajiv this morning. i have ensured they are aware of the advance care directive which remains in force and which specifies no hospital transfer and no resuscitation. this directive was reviewed with the family in november and all family members remain in agreement. i have ensured the directive is in the front of feekras file and at the bedside and that all nursing and medical staff know it is in place.

mev nambiar room 15 okay this is mev nambiar room fifteen two pm start of afternoon shift and i am completing a comprehensive assessment now. mev is eighty two years old she has advanced alzheimers dementia severe stage, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease gold stage three, type two diabetes managed with oral agents only as she is not on insulin, hypertension, and chronic constipation. her regular medications include fostair two puffs twice daily via aerochamber, spiriva respimat one puff once daily, salbutamol two puffs as required up to four hourly, metfomin five hundred milligrams twice daily with meals, sitagliptin one hundred milligrams once daily, amlodipine five milligrams once daily, and macrogol powder one sachet twice daily for her constipation. mev had an extremely difficult and complex morning and i will work through each issue systematically. the first and most serious issue is that mev attempted to climb over the right side cot rail at approximately eleven forty five this morning while the healthcare assistant deirdre was momentarily out of the room to collect fresh towels. deirdre heard a noise and returned to find mev with her right leg over the top of the cot rail and her upper body angled dangerously over the edge. deirdre called immediately for assistance and my colleague attracta attended within seconds. together they were able to manoeuvre mev safely back onto the mattress. mev did not fall and no injury was identified on immediate assessment. however this was a very serious and dangerous situation. mev is seventy four kilograms and the drop from the cot rail to the floor is approximately eighty centimetres. a fall from this height with her osteoporosis risk would carry a very high probability of hip or wrist fracture. this incident is the third time in eight days that mev has attempted to go over or through the cot side barriers. the incident has been fully documented as a patient safety event and reported to the unit manager and the on call physician dr walsh who reviewed mev at twelve thirty. following a full multidisciplinary discussion involving the unit manager attracta myself the physiotherapist and the occupational therapist we have agreed to implement the following immediate safety measures. first, crash mats have been placed on both sides of the bed and at the foot of the bed. second, a direct observation protocol is in place requiring a staff member to be visible and within arm's reach of mev at all times during waking hours for the next forty eight hours pending formal assessment. third, a formal restraint and least restrictive practice review is being scheduled for friday morning with all relevant disciplines present. any further physical restraint beyond the existing low-set cot sides would require a formal best interest assessment with family involvement and multidisciplinary team agreement under the unit's restraint minimisation policy and i have initiated this process by contacting mev's daughter chitra who is her next of kin and healthcare decision-maker. chitra has been informed of this morning's event and of the proposed review and she will attend on friday. mev's respiratory condition has deteriorated today compared to her recent assessment. her respiratory rate at two pm is twenty six breaths per minute. she has audible expiratory wheeze bilaterally and is using accessory muscles on exertion. her oxygen saturations on room air are ninety one percent which is below her usual baseline of ninety four to ninety five percent on room air. i have commenced two litres per minute of oxygen via nasal cannula and her saturations have improved to ninety four percent on supplemental oxygen. she has administered her salbutamol inhaler six times since seven am according to the morning documentation which is three times her usual daily usage frequency. her temperature at two pm is thirty seven point nine celsius. she has a productive cough today which is new compared to her recent baseline dry cough. she is producing green-yellow mucopurulent sputum on each coughing episode. i have taken a sputum sample for culture and sensitivity and have spoken with dr walsh who agrees that a lower respiratory tract infection is likely and who has prescribed doxycycline one hundred milligrams twice daily pending culture results. the antibiotic was given at two pm today. i have also commenced the respiratory observation chart with two hourly recording of respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, and breath sounds. mevs behavioural symptoms of dementia have been severe today. she has been continuously restless calling out mammy mammy mammy over and over and tearing at her clothing and bedding. on three occasions she succeeded in completely removing her hospital gown and was sitting undressed in bed. i have dressed her each time and have now put her in a wrap-around fastening gown which is more difficult for her to remove unassisted. the music therapy intervention using rte lyric fm which normally settles her within ten to fifteen minutes has been only partially effective today and i believe this is because she is physically unwell with the chest infection and her discomfort is overriding her response to the usual calming interventions. i have spoken with dr walsh about the possibility of a small dose of lorazepam for acute severe agitation if non-pharmacological measures fail but dr walsh wants to hold off until we have assessed the impact of the antibiotic and see whether her agitation reduces as her physical symptoms improve. this plan is documented. mevs oral intake today is very poor. her breakfast was untouched. at lunch she accepted two spoonfuls of yogurt and one sip of thickened fluid then turned her head away repeatedly despite multiple gentle attempts by the nursing staff to assist her. her two scheduled fortisip compact one-sixty millilitre drinks were both refused. i have documented her oral intake as critically reduced and have notified the dietitian. mevs last recorded weight was forty seven point three kilograms three days ago and she has been on dietitian-supervised nutritional management for six weeks due to a pattern of weight loss. the current episode of acute illness is likely to worsen the nutritional situation. i have asked the dietitian for urgent phone review today to advise on whether any escalation of nutritional support is appropriate within the goals of her dementia care plan and advance care directive which specifies no nasogastric or enteral feeding. mevs bowels opened this morning for the first time in three days and she passed a small amount of hard stool which is documented in the bowel chart. her macrogol dose is being continued.

roree okafor room 2 right so this is roree okafor in room two it is nine am tuesday morning start of early shift handover assessment. roree is a sixty eight year old male resident admitted eight months ago following a severe left middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke. he has significant residual left sided hemiplegia, mixed expressive and receptive dysphasia, right sided neglect, and cognitive impairment. he is the only male resident currently on this unit and as i have noted in previous assessments this creates specific considerations around privacy and dignity during intimate care. roree had a significant behavioural incident last night between approximately eleven fifty pm and two am according to the night documentation from cormac. the incident began when roree apparently became increasingly agitated in bed at around eleven thirty banging on his cot sides and vocalising loudly. cormac attended and assessed roree and found no obvious physical cause for the distress. roree was non-cooperative with assessment at that time. the agitation escalated over the next thirty minutes and at approximately midnight roree removed all of his clothing including his continence pad and stood at the doorway of his room naked shouting at the corridor. cormac attempted multiple times using verbal de-escalation and physical presence to guide roree back to bed but roree was resistive and remained at the doorway for approximately fifteen minutes. cormac called the on call physician dr patel who reviewed roree at twelve twenty and assessed him as acutely agitated with possible nocturnal delirium superimposed on his chronic cognitive impairment. dr patel prescribed lorazepam one milligram orally as a one-off dose and this was administered by cormac at twelve forty. roree gradually settled over the next forty minutes and was back in bed by approximately one thirty. cormac documented that roree slept soundly from one thirty until his eight am review. the cause of last night's agitation is not entirely clear. possible contributing factors include pain from his spastic left arm which has been more contractured lately, urinary discomfort as his catheter was noted by cormac to have a small kink at the leg bag connection at eleven pm which was corrected, possible environmental factors as there was significant noise in the corridor from another resident requiring emergency medical attention around that time, and the underlying neurological and cognitive changes from his stroke which predispose him to nocturnal delirium. i have documented all of these possible factors and will review with the multidisciplinary team at the tuesday afternoon case conference. the lorazepam was a one off prescription and no regular benzodiazepine is in place. dr patel has documented that if similar episodes recur the question of a low dose regular anxiolytic should be discussed at a multidisciplinary level and not prescribed in isolation. roree is now sleeping at nine am with regular unlaboured respirations at fourteen per minute. his colour is good and his lips are pink. i will allow him to sleep until ten am and then attempt to rouse him gently as he has a chest physiotherapy session booked for ten thirty with the physiotherapist fionnuala and this is important for his ongoing respiratory management and prevention of pneumonia which is his most serious post-stroke complication risk. his morning medications are scheduled at nine am but given that he is sleeping following sedation the prescribing team will need to advise on whether these should be given on waking or delayed to their normal time. i have documented this and put a call out to dr patel. roorees medication regime is very complex. he is on clopidogrel seventy five milligrams daily for secondary stroke prevention. atorvastatin forty milligrams at night for lipid management. ramipril ten milligrams daily for his hypertension and left ventricular systolic dysfunction. spironalactone fifty milligrams daily for his cardiac failure management. dapagliflozin ten milligrams daily for his type two diabetes and for the additional cardiac protective benefit in his cardiac failure. metfomin five hundred milligrams twice daily. linagliptin five milligrams daily. insullin degludec twenty units subcutaneously at bedtime. his morning blood glucose taken at eight thirty by cormac in the final hour of the night shift was eight point three millimoles per litre which is within his target range of six to ten millimoles per litre. his blood pressure taken at eight thirty was one hundred and forty five over eighty eight. the target set by the stroke consultant dr lyons is less than one hundred and thirty over eighty but achieving this has been very difficult in practice despite optimal medication management. roorees stroke rehabilitation has been progressing slowly. his physiotherapy goals are to maintain range of movement in the left shoulder and hip, to achieve transfers with a single person assist rather than the current two person hoist, and to achieve some left leg weight bearing during standing transfers. his occupational therapy goals focus on maximising independence in upper limb activities including self-feeding with adapted utensils on the right side, and on cognitive rehabilitation including attention and memory tasks. the speech and language therapist last reviewed him five months ago. at that time his dysphagia was assessed as safe for normal textures with mildly thickened fluids and his communication was assessed using the aphasia impact questionnaire. given the amount of time that has elapsed and his changing clinical picture i have submitted a new referral to speech and language today for both swallowing reassessment and communication strategy review. roorees catheter is a long-term silicone urethral catheter size sixteen french inserted six weeks ago for urinary retention secondary to neurogenic bladder from the stroke. it was last changed three weeks ago and is due for its next change in three weeks. the urine today is clear and pale yellow with no signs of blockage or infection. there is no catheter-associated discomfort documented. the leg bag is clean and the junction is secure. his personal care today will require full assistance including washing dressing oral care and catheter hygiene. he soiled his continence pad overnight and this has been changed by cormac. full skin integrity assessment will be completed as part of morning care.

leesha iyer room 6 this is leesha iyer room six afternoon assessment it is four thirty pm friday. leesha arrived as an emergency admission yesterday afternoon from her home following a fall in her kitchen which was witnessed by her neighbour mrs hennessy who found leesha on the kitchen floor in a semiconscious state at approximately two forty five pm. mrs hennessy called nine nine nine and leesha was brought by ambulance to the emergency department at mater hospital. the history obtained from mrs hennessy and from leesha herself when she became more alert in the emergency department indicates that leesha fell while stepping backward from her kitchen counter and struck the back of her head on the edge of the table. the period of loss of consciousness was estimated by mrs hennessy as being between fifteen and thirty minutes although this is an estimate and not a precise measurement. leesha is eighty four years old and lives alone. she has no family in ireland. her nearest relative is her nephew vijay iyer who lives in london and who has been contacted by phone. vijay is travelling to ireland by plane tomorrow morning and expects to arrive here by midday. i have confirmed vijays contact details and next of kin status in the admission documentation. in the emergency department leesha underwent computed tomography of the head without contrast which was reported by the radiologist as showing no acute intracranial haemorrhage and no skull fracture. however a small chronic subdural hygroma is noted on the right side measuring approximately four millimetres at its widest point. the radiologist has documented this as likely a chronic finding and recommends follow up imaging in six weeks unless clinical deterioration occurs. the neurosurgical registrar was contacted by the emergency department who advised conservative management with close neurological monitoring given the absence of acute bleed and the stability of the hygroma. the laceration to the occipital area measuring approximately three point five centimetres in length was closed with four metallic staples in the emergency department. the wound has been assessed today and is clean and dry with no surrounding erythema or discharge and all four staples are intact and appropriately positioned. wound review and staple removal is planned for day ten which would be next monday. leesha is on warfrin for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. the exact dose was not documented in the transfer paperwork from the emergency department but i contacted her gp dr patil this morning and confirmed the current dose as five milligrams on monday wednesday friday and sunday and four milligrams on tuesday thursday and saturday. her inr was checked on admission yesterday and returned at three point nine. this is supratherapeutic above her target range of two to three and is a significant clinical concern in the context of a head injury with a known subdural hygroma even though the hygroma appears chronic. dr costello reviewed this result and has prescribed warfrin to be withheld for three days with a repeat inr at seventy two hours. the plan is to reassess anticoagulation once the inr has come down into therapeutic range and once the clinical situation has been further evaluated. no vitamin k or prothrombin complex concentrate has been prescribed as the decision was made to manage conservatively given the normal ct findings, the absence of neurological deterioration, and the haemostatic pressure dressing applied to the wound. this management plan and its rationale is clearly documented in the medical notes and in the nursing care plan. i have ensured that the specific instruction that leesha must be immediately transferred to hospital by emergency ambulance if any neurological deterioration is detected is written at the front of her nursing care plan and communicated verbally at every shift handover. neurological observations are being performed every two hours and the results are documented on the standardised neuro obs chart. at the four pm observation leesha's glasgow coma scale is fourteen out of fifteen. she loses one point on the orientation section believing today to be wednesday rather than friday and believing she is in a hospital rather than in a care facility. she is fully oriented to person. her pupils are three millimetres bilaterally equal and reactive to light both direct and consensual. her grip strength is symmetric bilaterally and her plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. she has no facial asymmetry. she reports a residual headache which she rates at two to three out of ten today compared to five to six out of ten yesterday morning. she was given paracetamol one gram orally at two pm and has found this helpful for the headache. blood pressure at four pm is one hundred and forty four over eighty. pulse is sixty eight per minute and regular. respiratory rate is fifteen per minute. temperature is thirty six point seven celsius. leesha has a bilateral essential tremor which she tells me has been present for approximately twenty years and is well documented in her gp notes. the tremor is more prominent in her hands when she is anxious and it has been noticeable today particularly when she is trying to hold her cup of tea. i have provided a weighted cup and a non-slip mat on her over-bed table which has helped her manage independently. she is fully continent and has been mobilising independently with her own walking stick inside her room. she walked approximately fifteen metres in the corridor this afternoon with supervision from me and managed this without stumbling or stopping. the physiotherapist marie has been asked to provide a formal mobility assessment and falls risk reassessment tomorrow morning. leesha is very anxious and distressed by the unfamiliar environment. she repeatedly expresses that she is frightened of what might happen to her and whether she will be able to return to her own home. i have sat with her twice today for fifteen to twenty minutes each time providing reassurance and information at a pace she can manage. she is very sharp cognitively when not acutely distressed and is able to retain and process information well when presented calmly. i have explained the plan regarding the inr monitoring, the head injury observation protocol, and the physiotherapy assessment to her satisfaction. i have also arranged for the social worker saoirse to visit tomorrow to begin an assessment of what home support package leesha might need if she is to return home safely. leesha has eaten reasonably today. she took most of her lunch and accepted about half of her tea. she is taking fluids independently but needs reminders. she is on no diabetes medication but i have noted her blood glucose was eight point four millimoles per litre post lunch today which is slightly elevated though she is not a known diabetic. i will flag this for the doctor's attention at review tomorrow.

conker oconnell room 11 this is conker oconnell in room eleven morning assessment it is nine fifteen am on monday. conker is seventy six years old he has moderate stage parkinson's disease with parkinson's disease dementia confirmed approximately fourteen months ago by the memory clinic at beaumont hospital. he also has ischaemic heart disease with two previous myocardial infarctions, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, and orthostatic hypotension which is a direct complication of both the parkinson's disease and several of his medications. his medication regime is extremely complex and timing-critical. sinemet one hundred and twenty five milligrams is prescribed four times daily at the very specific times of six am ten am two pm and six pm and any deviation from these times can cause significant motor deterioration. madopar dispersible sixty two point five milligrams is prescribed as a rescue dose for breakthrough motor off episodes. ropinirole prolonged release four milligrams is prescribed once daily at bedtime. domperidone ten milligrams three times daily thirty minutes before meals for his gastroparesis. omeprazole twenty milligrams once daily for the gastro-oesophageal reflux. aspirin seventy five milligrams once daily as antiplatelet therapy. bisoprolol two point five milligrams once daily for his heart failure. furosemide twenty milligrams once daily for mild fluid retention. the timing of the sinemet and madopar doses is the single most important aspect of conker's nursing management and i want to be very clear in this record that the six am dose of sinemet was administered at exactly six am this morning by the night nurse eimear who specifically documents this. the nine am medications were given at nine am exactly by me this morning and i will ensure the two pm and six pm doses are given at their precise times as delays even of thirty to forty five minutes can result in conker entering a severe motor off state characterised by freezing, rigidity, inability to walk, and extreme distress. conker had a very difficult night. eimear's night documentation describes multiple episodes of vivid and frightening nightmares. conker woke screaming on at least three occasions and was found sitting upright in bed in obvious distress. on one occasion he appeared to be striking out at an unseen person in the room and eimear had to take care not to be struck when she approached. when he became more alert he described very detailed dreams about being chased and about his family being harmed. these dreams were extremely vivid and convincing to him and he took some time on each occasion to orient himself to reality after waking. this pattern is consistent with rapid eye movement sleep behaviour disorder which is a well recognised complication of parkinson's disease and parkinson's disease dementia. eimear sat with conker after each episode for approximately twenty minutes until he resettled. the dreams are not new but eimear notes they appear to have increased in frequency and intensity over the past week. i have reviewed the clinical notes and have confirmed that the consultant neurologist dr byrne discussed this issue at conker's last review three months ago and documented a plan to consider melatonin two milligrams at bedtime as a first line treatment for the rem sleep behaviour disorder but that the prescription was not written at that visit. i have contacted dr byrne's secretary to request that this prescription be issued and i have also written a query in the medical notes requesting a response from the ward medical team. this morning conker is in a significant motor off state. his tremor in the right hand is severe and rhythmic at approximately five to six hertz making any fine motor task such as brushing teeth, holding cutlery, or buttoning clothing impossible without full assistance. he has experienced three freezing episodes at doorway transitions this morning between seven and nine fifteen. on each occasion i used the laser cue wristband device which projects a red line on the floor and which conker finds more reliably effective than the sticky tape threshold method. two of the three episodes responded within thirty to sixty seconds to the laser cue. the third episode lasted approximately four minutes despite the cue and i also used auditory cueing by saying left, left, left in a rhythmic pattern which eventually triggered the step. conker was visibly distressed and frustrated by the duration of this third freezing episode and banged his hand on the door frame in frustration. i acknowledged his frustration verbally and reassured him that we would work on this together. his voice today is very hypophonic and mildly dysarthric. conversational speech is difficult to hear even at close range and i need to ask him to repeat himself several times. i have noted that this is slightly worse than last week and will document it in the communications section for the speech and language therapist to review at the next session. his blood pressure lying at nine am is one hundred and eight over sixty four. sitting blood pressure taken after two minutes in an upright seated position is eighty nine over fifty eight. this represents a drop of nineteen millimetres of mercury systolic on position change which meets the definition of orthostatic hypotension and is a significant fall risk. i have advised conker never to get up from bed or chair without pressing the call bell and have ensured all staff are verbally briefed this morning that no transfers should occur without a nurse present and that all position changes must be performed very slowly with a two to three minute sit-on-edge pause before standing. i have documented this in the nursing care plan. his oral intake this morning was poor. he managed half a bowl of porridge and one cup of tea. his drooling and hypersalivation is very prominent today and he finds it very humiliating. he had to wipe his face multiple times during breakfast and became tearful and said he did not want anyone to see him like this. i sat with him and provided extra napkins and worked at a very slow unhurried pace to try to reduce his embarrassment. the speech and language therapist had previously recommended glycopyrrolate as a medical treatment for the hypersalivation but dr byrne had not issued the prescription. i have again added this to the outstanding medical review query list. conker's weight today is sixty three point zero kilograms which is down from sixty three point eight kilograms one week ago. this is a loss of eight hundred grams in seven days which is clinically significant and consistent with his poor oral intake pattern. i have updated the malnutrition universal screening tool score and the dietitian has been notified.

tierney thomas room 8B this is tierney thomas room eight b it is one pm thursday palliative care assessment. tierney is a ninety one year old woman with end stage congestive cardiac failure new york heart association class four, chronic kidney disease stage four, type two diabetes, and severe osteoporosis with documented vertebral compression fractures at thoracic six and lumbar one. she is being managed under a fully established palliative care framework with a comprehensive advance care directive signed and witnessed in september of last year. the directive specifies no hospital transfer, no cardiopulmonary resuscitation, no invasive procedures including no nasogastric or intravenous feeding, no blood taking beyond what is needed for immediate comfort management, and no escalation of treatment for acute deterioration. the family specifically tierneys daughter siobhan thomas and her son declan thomas are fully informed and completely supportive of this framework. both have expressed on multiple occasions that they want tierney to die in comfort and dignity in this facility surrounded by her family and the staff who know her. i met with siobhan this morning at ten thirty for a formal family meeting lasting approximately fifty minutes. siobhan raised several concerns and observations that i want to document carefully. she has noticed over the past week that her mothers breathing has changed significantly. she describes it as more laboured with longer pauses and a different pattern from before. she also described hearing a rattling or gurgling sound in tierneys chest when she visits which she says began approximately three or four days ago. she asked me directly whether her mother is dying. i answered this question honestly and compassionately. i told her that yes i believe tierney is entering the final phase of her life, that the changes she is observing including the breathing pattern and the secretions are consistent with the dying process, and that the medical and nursing team are focused entirely on keeping tierney comfortable and free from pain and distress. siobhan cried during this part of our conversation and i provided tissues and sat with her quietly for several minutes. she then said she was grateful for the honesty and said she had been feeling she needed permission to acknowledge what she was seeing. declan has been less available due to work commitments but siobhan has been sharing information with him by phone. i have offered both siblings access to the pastoral care coordinator and the bereavement support service and siobhan has said she would like the chaplain to visit today if possible. i have contacted the chaplain who will visit at four pm. on my one pm clinical assessment tierney is lying at thirty degrees elevation in bed. her eyes are partially open but she does not track movement or respond to her name consistently. she occasionally opens her eyes briefly when her hand is held and squeezed gently which i interpret as a retained awareness at some level. her breathing today shows a very marked cheyne stokes pattern with crescendo phases lasting approximately twenty seconds followed by apnoeic pauses of eight to fifteen seconds. this is more pronounced than at yesterday's assessment when the apnoeic pauses were shorter at five to eight seconds. i have documented this progression. dr egan the palliative care consultant was consulted by phone this morning and reviewed his notes from his in-person visit on wednesday. he has increased the subcutaneous morphine in the continuous syringe driver from twenty to twenty five milligrams over twenty four hours to address the visible respiratory distress and subtle facial grimacing seen during the crescendo breathing phases. he has also increased the midazolam in the syringe driver from two point five milligrams to five milligrams over twenty four hours to address the restlessness and agitation that has been escalating over the past forty eight hours. the syringe driver was reloaded with the new doses at twelve noon today by the palliative care clinical nurse specialist catherine who made an unplanned visit to the unit specifically to assist with this management adjustment. i have confirmed that the syringe driver is running correctly at one pm with the correct medications and doses loaded and the site is clean. the secretions which siobhan described as rattling are audible in the room at about thirty centimetres distance from tierneys face. they are wet secretions caused by the inability to clear saliva and bronchial secretions due to the progressive loss of muscle tone and swallow reflex. hyoscine butylbromide twenty milligrams subcutaneously was administered at nine am and again at twelve noon today as prescribed for secretion management. at one pm the secretions are present but less audible than at nine am suggesting some response to the hyoscine. i have carefully explained to siobhan and separately to declan by phone that the secretion noise does not indicate that tierney is in distress or struggling to breathe and that the sounds are much more distressing for observers than they are for the patient herself. this is well evidenced in the palliative care literature and both family members received this explanation with some degree of relief. tierneys oral care is being provided every two hours using pink oral sponge sticks moistened with water. her lips are cracked and dry and i have been applying a thick barrier lip balm after each oral care session. her oral mucosa shows some white patches which i have swabbed and sent for culture to rule out oral candidiasis which is common in the dying phase. nystatin oral suspension is prescribed and will be commenced if candida is confirmed. tierney has not taken any food or fluid orally for four days now. she has had no urine output for the past eighteen hours and the long term urethral catheter which has been in place for three months shows no drainage. i have flushed the catheter gently with ten millilitres of normal saline to ensure it is not blocked and confirmed the catheter remains in the bladder but the kidneys appear to have essentially ceased functioning. this is an expected and recognised finding in the final hours to days of life and is consistent with the profound multi-organ failure she is experiencing. i have documented this and informed the family that this represents a further sign that tierney is very close to the end of her life. the family are staying close and siobhan has asked whether she should bring declan tonight in case tierney does not survive until morning and i have gently said that yes if declan is able to be there i would encourage it. this conversation has been documented.

sadhbh menon room 4 this is sadhbv menon in room four three pm wednesday afternoon assessment. sadhbv is seventy nine years old she has type one diabetes mellitus managed on a basal bolus insulin regime, hypertension, chronic atrial fibrillation anticoagulated with apixaban, chronic kidney disease stage three b, a recent diagnosis of moderate frailty syndrome, and a history of five documented urinary tract infections in the past twelve months for which she is on prophylactic trimethoprim one hundred milligrams at night. today has been a very complex day and i have multiple significant clinical events to document. the first and most serious event is that sadhbv self removed her intravenous cannula from her left forearm at approximately one pm today. she was in the process of receiving intravenous normal saline at one hundred millilitres per hour which was prescribed yesterday by dr ahmed to address dehydration secondary to reduced oral intake over the past five days with clinical signs including dry mucous membranes, skin turgor changes, concentrated urine, and a rise in her creatinine from her baseline of one hundred and twenty five to one hundred and fifty two micromoles per litre on monday's bloods. i was out of the room briefly attending to another resident when this occurred. when i returned at approximately one oh five i found the intravenous line disconnected and dripping onto the mattress and the cannula lying on the bed beside sadhbvs left arm. sadhbv was sitting calmly and eating a digestive biscuit. she stated that the drip had fallen out by itself. however the appearance of the insertion site is not consistent with an accidental dislodgement. there is a tear mark at the inferior edge of the insertion site measuring approximately two millimetres which is characteristic of the skin tear pattern seen with forcible self removal rather than the clean symmetric appearance typical of a cannula that has slipped out spontaneously. this is the second episode of cannula self removal in this admission. the first occurred last thursday at approximately eleven am. on that occasion sadhbv was much more forthright about her actions and told me that she had never wanted the drip and that she did not believe she needed it and that it was uncomfortable and limiting her movement. she refused reinsertion for approximately six hours that day before eventually relenting and agreeing to reinsertion at five pm following a lengthy conversation with dr ahmed who explained the clinical reasoning in detail. i have documented today's event as a patient self-removal of intravenous access, completed a clinical incident report form, and contacted dr ahmed. dr ahmed has reviewed the situation by phone and has decided that given sadhbvs fluctuating cooperation with intravenous therapy and the fact that her repeat bloods drawn this morning show her creatinine at one hundred and forty five micromoles per litre which is a modest improvement from monday's one hundred and fifty two and is only marginally above her personal baseline, he is comfortable to step down the rehydration strategy to aggressive oral hydration with a target of two thousand millilitres per day and to repeat biochemistry tomorrow morning. if her renal function deteriorates or her oral intake remains inadequate he will reconsider whether to reinsert intravenous access and will have a more formal best interest discussion with sadhbv at that point. i have explained this plan to sadhbv and encouraged her strongly to try to drink more today. she has said she will try. her blood glucose management today has been very problematic. her fasting blood glucose at seven am was three point one millimoles per litre which meets the formal definition of hypoglycaemia below three point nine. she was symptomatic with trembling, sweating, and mild confusion at that time. i administered fifteen grams of rapid-acting carbohydrate in the form of one hundred and fifty millilitres of fresh orange juice at seven ten am and rechecked her glucose at seven twenty five obtaining five point two millimoles per litre which is satisfactory. i confirmed she was symptom free before proceeding with the morning insulin. her novorapid aspart eight units was administered at seven thirty with breakfast. her pre-lunch glucose at twelve noon was eighteen point six millimoles per litre which is significantly hyperglycaemic. this dramatic swing from hypoglycaemia at seven am to hyperglycaemia at noon in the context of variable food intake and the fluid and metabolic stress of her acute illness represents a very unstable glycaemic pattern. the diabetes specialist nurse siobhan has reviewed her by phone today and has recommended temporary dose reductions as follows. breakfast novorapid reduced from eight units to four units, lunch novorapid reduced from eight units to four units, dinner novorapid reduced from eight units to six units, lantus glargine to remain at twenty two units at bedtime. these adjustments have been verbally authorised by dr ahmed and i have documented them clearly in the medication administration record with the clinical rationale. the adjusted breakfast novorapid of four units was given at seven thirty with breakfast. the adjusted lunch novorapid of four units was given at twelve thirty with lunch. i will give the adjusted dinner novorapid of six units at approximately six pm. her three pm blood glucose is eleven point four millimoles per litre which is an improvement from the noon reading but still above target. i will continue to monitor hourly until she is consistently in the six to ten range. sadhbv also has a new skin tear on her right forearm that i discovered during her afternoon personal care at two thirty pm. the skin tear measures approximately four point five centimetres in length and approximately one point five centimetres at its widest point. it is a category two skin tear with a partial flap present. the wound edges are clean and there is minimal bleeding controlled with direct pressure. i have irrigated with normal saline, realigned the partial flap as best as possible, and applied a silicone foam bordered dressing to maintain moisture balance and protect the fragile surrounding skin. the right forearm has been elevated on a pillow to reduce oedema. sadhbv cannot recall injuring herself which is not unusual for skin tears in frail elderly people as the skin is so fragile that minor unnoticed contact can cause a tear. she is on apixaban which will prolong any bleeding tendency and this is documented in the wound chart as a relevant factor. i have completed a full skin integrity assessment today. her arms, shins, and dorsal hands all show the characteristic changes of skin fragility and the care plan has been updated to include the following protective measures. long sleeve loose cotton garments at all times. padded arm guards to be worn while in the wheelchair. all jewellery removed from wrists and hands permanently during her stay. bed rails and wheelchair armrests to be padded with foam protectors. staff to always use slide sheets for repositioning and never pull on limbs. i have communicated all of these measures verbally at the nursing afternoon standup and they are documented in the care plan. sadhbvs renal function in the context of today's events is being managed with the oral hydration strategy as above. her urine output from seven am to three pm today is three hundred and ten millilitres which is low. her urine is dark yellow and concentrated. i am closely monitoring the fluid balance chart and will contact dr ahmed if output does not improve by the six pm check or if her clinical status deteriorates in any way. her atrial fibrillation is rate-controlled at approximately eighty to ninety beats per minute and her last echocardiogram six months ago showed moderate left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction. apixaban two point five milligrams twice daily was administered this morning and is due again at six pm. her blood pressure at three pm is one hundred and forty eight over eighty four which is within acceptable range for her managed hypertension target of less than one hundred and fifty over ninety.
